title: Concurrency and Threads
summary: Write concurrent Python code using threads, asyncio, and multiprocessing

sections.title: Concurrency is like cooking multiple dishes
sections.context: Imagine cooking a three-course meal. One approach: cook the appetizer, finish it, cook the main, finish it, cook dessert — sequential. Faster approach: while the main course is in the oven (I/O wait), prep the dessert ingredients. Python offers three concurrency models. Threading (threading module) is good for I/O-bound tasks — waiting for network responses, file reads, database queries. Multiprocessing (multiprocessing module) bypasses the GIL by using separate processes, ideal for CPU-bound work like image processing. Asyncio (asyncio module) provides cooperative multitasking for high I/O concurrency with a single thread — single-threaded but non-blocking.

sections.title: Threading for I/O-bound network requests
sections.content: Download multiple web pages concurrently instead of sequentially
sections.code:
import threading  # For running tasks in parallel threads
import time  # For measuring duration and simulating delays
import urllib.request  # For making HTTP requests to URLs

def download_url(url):
    # Simulate downloading a webpage
    print(f"Starting: {url}")  # Log when thread begins
    # urllib.request.urlopen fetches the URL content
    response = urllib.request.urlopen(url)  # This blocks while waiting
    data = response.read()  # Read the response body
    print(f"Finished: {url} ({len(data)} bytes)")  # Log completion

# List of URLs to download
urls = [
    "https://www.python.org",
    "https://www.wikipedia.org",
    "https://www.github.com",
]

start = time.time()  # Record the start time

# Create and start a thread for each URL — they run concurrently
threads = []  # Keep track of all created threads
for url in urls:
    t = threading.Thread(target=download_url, args=(url,))
    threads.append(t)  # Store for later joining
    t.start()  # Start the thread — it begins executing

# Wait for all threads to complete before continuing
for t in threads:
    t.join()  # Blocks until this thread finishes

print(f"Total time: {time.time() - start:.2f}s")

sections.title: The GIL, asyncio, and multiprocessing — choosing the right tool
sections.context: The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is Python's most infamous limitation: only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time. This means threading doesn't speed up CPU-bound tasks (like calculating prime numbers). For I/O-bound work, threads are fine because the GIL is released during I/O waits. For CPU-bound work, use multiprocessing — each process has its own GIL and Python interpreter. Asyncio provides coroutines (async/await) for high-concurrency I/O without threads or processes — best for thousands of simultaneous connections (web servers, chat applications). Rule of thumb: I/O-bound + many connections = asyncio; I/O-bound + few connections = threading; CPU-bound = multiprocessing.

sections.title: Thread safety — shared data needs locks
sections.content: Multiple threads modifying the same variable creates race conditions. Wrong: counter += 1 in two threads at once — the increment isn't atomic and loses updates. Correct: use threading.Lock to synchronize access: with lock: counter += 1. For asyncio, use asyncio.Lock. For multiprocessing, use multiprocessing.Lock. Even simple operations aren't safe without synchronization.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Write a program that uses multiprocessing to calculate the sum of squares for numbers 1 to 10,000,000. Split the work evenly across 4 processes (each process handles 2,500,000 numbers). Use multiprocessing.Pool or multiprocessing.Process to parallelize. Compare the execution time vs a single-threaded version.
hints: How do you split a large range into equal chunks for each process?
Which multiprocessing class lets you distribute tasks across worker processes?
